events.title: Badarian and Naqada Cultures
events.detail: Predynastic communities in the Nile Valley develop sophisticated pottery, agriculture, and early burial customs. The Naqada II culture (Gerzean) exhibits long-distance trade, proto-hieroglyphic symbols, and early centralization. These foundations precede pharaonic Egypt by several centuries.

sources.name: Kemp, Barry — Ancient Egypt: Anatomy of a Civilization (Routledge, 2006)
British Museum Predynastic Egypt collections

events.title: Narmer Unifies the Two Lands
events.detail: King Narmer (sometimes identified with Menes) unifies Upper and Lower Egypt, establishing the First Dynasty. The Narmer Palette — a ceremonial slate tableau — depicts the conquest and is considered the world's first historical document. Memphis becomes the first capital.

sources.name: Narmer Palette (Egyptian Museum, Cairo)
Shaw, Ian (ed.) — Oxford History of Ancient Egypt (OUP, 2000)

events.title: Hieroglyphic Writing System Emerges
events.detail: The world's earliest coherent writing system appears in Egypt — hieroglyphics carved on ivory labels at Abydos predate Mesopotamian cuneiform tablets in some assessments. Combined logograms, phonograms, and determinatives encode a full language and administrative record-keeping system.

sources.name: Dreyer, Günter — Umm el-Qaab I: Das prädynastische Königsgrab U-j (DAI, 1998)
Fischer, Henry — The Orientation of Hieroglyphs (MMA, 1977)

events.title: Step Pyramid of Djoser Built at Saqqara
events.detail: Pharaoh Djoser's architect Imhotep designs the first monumental stone structure in history — a six-tiered step pyramid at Saqqara rising 62 meters. Imhotep, later deified, is also regarded as the father of medicine. The complex redefines Egyptian funerary architecture.

sources.name: Lauer, Jean-Philippe — Saqqara: The Royal Cemetery of Memphis (Thames & Hudson, 1976)
UNESCO World Heritage Listing — Memphis and its Necropolis

era: Old Kingdom — Age of Pyramids (2686–2181 BCE)

events.title: Great Pyramid of Khufu Completed at Giza
events.detail: The Great Pyramid of Khufu (Cheops) — 146.5 meters tall, containing 2.3 million stone blocks averaging 2.5 tons — stands as one of the Seven Wonders of the Ancient World and the only one surviving. Built by a workforce of tens of thousands over approximately 20 years, the project showcases extraordinary organizational and engineering capacity.

sources.name: Lehner, Mark — The Complete Pyramids (Thames & Hudson, 1997)
Hawass, Zahi — The Pyramids of Ancient Egypt (Carnegie Museum, 1990)
UNESCO World Heritage — Pyramid Fields from Giza to Dahshur

events.title: Great Sphinx of Giza Carved
events.detail: The Great Sphinx — 73 meters long, 20 meters tall, carved from a single limestone outcrop — is constructed during the reign of Khafre (or possibly Khufu). The monolith, depicting a lion with a human head, guards the Giza necropolis and remains one of humanity's largest statues. Its nose was reportedly destroyed in the medieval period.

sources.name: Hawass, Zahi — The Secrets of the Sphinx (AUC Press, 1998)
Stadelmann, Rainer — Die ägyptischen Pyramiden (von Zabern, 1991)

events.title: First Intermediate Period — Collapse of Old Kingdom
events.detail: The Old Kingdom collapses amid drought, famine, declining Nile floods, and fragmentation of central authority. Regional nomarchs (governors) gain independence; the country splits into competing centers at Herakleopolis and Thebes. Historical records describe social upheaval — the Ipuwer Papyrus laments the inversion of social order.

sources.name: Lichtheim, Miriam — Ancient Egyptian Literature Vol. 1 (UC Press, 1973)
Moreno Garcia, Juan Carlos — L'administration provinciale dans l'Égypte de l'Ancien Empire (CRIPEL, 1999)

events.title: Mentuhotep II Reunifies Egypt
events.detail: Theban king Mentuhotep II defeats the Herakleopolitan dynasty and reunifies Egypt, founding the Middle Kingdom. He launches campaigns into Nubia, Libya, and the Sinai. His mortuary temple at Deir el-Bahari establishes a new architectural tradition. This period sees a flowering of literature, including the Tale of Sinuhe.

sources.name: Lichtheim, Miriam — Ancient Egyptian Literature Vol. 1 (UC Press, 1973)
Grimal, Nicolas — A History of Ancient Egypt (Blackwell, 1992)

events.title: Hyksos Invade and Occupy Lower Egypt
events.detail: The Hyksos — Semitic-speaking migrants from the Levant — seize the Delta region and establish the 15th Dynasty at Avaris (modern Tell el-Dab'a). They introduce the horse-drawn chariot, composite bow, and bronze weapons to Egypt. Their rule of roughly 100 years represents the first foreign occupation of Egypt.

sources.name: Redford, Donald — Egypt, Canaan, and Israel in Ancient Times (Princeton UP, 1992)
Bietak, Manfred — Avaris: The Capital of the Hyksos (British Museum, 1996)

events.title: Ahmose I Expels the Hyksos — New Kingdom Founded
events.detail: Pharaoh Ahmose I of Thebes besieges and captures Avaris, expelling the Hyksos and founding the New Kingdom (18th Dynasty). He pursues them into the Levant and raids Nubia, establishing Egypt as a Mediterranean and Near Eastern empire. The New Kingdom becomes Egypt's most powerful and internationally active period.

sources.name: Redford, Donald — History and Chronology of the 18th Dynasty (U of Toronto, 1967)
Grimal, Nicolas — A History of Ancient Egypt (Blackwell, 1992)

events.title: Hatshepsut Rules as Pharaoh
events.detail: Hatshepsut — initially regent for her stepson Thutmose III — assumes full pharaonic titles and rules Egypt for approximately 20 years as one of its most successful monarchs. She launches a famous trading expedition to the land of Punt, constructs the elegant mortuary temple at Deir el-Bahari, and oversees a period of prosperity and architectural achievement.

sources.name: Tyldesley, Joyce — Hatchepsut: The Female Pharaoh (Viking, 1996)
Metropolitan Museum of Art — Hatshepsut: From Queen to Pharaoh (2005)

events.title: Akhenaten's Monotheistic Revolution — Amarna Period
events.detail: Pharaoh Amenhotep IV renames himself Akhenaten ('Effective for the Aten'), bans the traditional polytheistic pantheon, and moves the capital to a new city, Akhetaten (modern Amarna). He introduces worship of the Aten (solar disc) as the sole god. His queen Nefertiti becomes a powerful co-ruler. This religious revolution is reversed after his death.

sources.name: Redford, Donald — Akhenaten: The Heretic King (Princeton UP, 1984)
Aldred, Cyril — Akhenaten: King of Egypt (Thames & Hudson, 1988)

events.title: Tutankhamun Restores Traditional Religion
events.detail: The young pharaoh Tutankhamun — likely son of Akhenaten — abandons the Amarna heresy, restores polytheism, and returns the capital to Memphis and later Thebes. He dies aged ~18 under mysterious circumstances. His intact tomb, discovered by Howard Carter in 1922, revolutionizes understanding of New Kingdom Egypt.

sources.name: Carter, Howard & Mace, Arthur — The Tomb of Tut-Ankh-Amen (Cassell, 1923)
James, T.G.H. — Tutankhamun (Friedman/Fairfax, 1992)

events.title: Battle of Kadesh — First Peace Treaty
events.detail: Ramesses II leads Egypt against the Hittite Empire at Kadesh (modern Syria) in history's first recorded large-scale chariot battle. The outcome is inconclusive; both sides claim victory. In 1258 BCE, Ramesses II and Hittite king Hattusili III sign the earliest surviving peace treaty in history — the Egyptian-Hittite Treaty, copies held at the UN today.

sources.name: Kitchen, K.A. — Pharaoh Triumphant: The Life and Times of Ramesses II (Aris & Phillips, 1982)
United Nations — Egyptian-Hittite Treaty replica (UN Headquarters, New York)

events.title: Abu Simbel Temples Completed by Ramesses II
events.detail: Ramesses II completes the twin rock-cut temples at Abu Simbel in Nubia: the Great Temple dedicated to himself and the gods, and the Small Temple for his queen Nefertari. Four 20-meter colossal statues of Ramesses II guard the entrance. The temples are precisely aligned so sunlight illuminates the inner sanctuary twice yearly.

sources.name: UNESCO World Heritage — Nubian Monuments from Abu Simbel to Philae (1979)
MacQuitty, William — Abu Simbel (Putnam, 1965)

events.title: Saite Renaissance — 26th Dynasty
events.detail: After Assyrian occupation of Lower Egypt, Psamtik I establishes the 26th (Saite) Dynasty and reunifies the country from Sais in the Delta. The Saite period sees a deliberate archaism — revival of Old Kingdom art styles — and opens Egypt to Greek mercenaries and traders who found the colony of Naucratis.

sources.name: Lloyd, Alan — Herodotus Book II Commentary (Brill, 1975)
Kienitz, Friedrich Karl — Die politische Geschichte Ägyptens (Akademie-Verlag, 1953)

events.title: Persian Conquest — Cambyses II Takes Egypt
events.detail: Achaemenid Persian king Cambyses II defeats pharaoh Psamtik III at the Battle of Pelusium, ending the 26th Dynasty. Egypt becomes the satrapy of Mudrāya. Persian rule continues intermittently until 332 BCE with nationalist revolts in between. The Persians are regarded as oppressive occupiers in Egyptian sources.

sources.name: Herodotus — Histories, Book III (c. 430 BCE)
Briant, Pierre — From Cyrus to Alexander: A History of the Persian Empire (Eisenbrauns, 2002)

events.title: Alexander the Great Conquers Egypt
events.detail: Alexander III of Macedon enters Egypt without resistance after his Levantine campaign. The Egyptians welcome him as a liberator from Persian rule; he is crowned pharaoh at Memphis and consults the Oracle of Amun at Siwa. He founds Alexandria on the Mediterranean coast in 331 BCE, destined to become the ancient world's foremost intellectual center.

sources.name: Arrian — Anabasis Alexandri (c. 150 CE)
Green, Peter — Alexander of Macedon: A Historical Biography (U of California, 1991)

era: Ptolemaic Egypt & Roman Province (305 BCE – 642 CE)

events.title: Ptolemy I Founds Ptolemaic Dynasty
events.detail: After Alexander's death, his general Ptolemy I Soter declares himself Pharaoh and founds the Ptolemaic dynasty. Alexandria becomes the world's leading city — home of the Great Library, the Pharos lighthouse (one of the Seven Wonders), and the Mouseion (proto-university). The Ptolemies blend Greek and Egyptian culture, issuing bilingual decrees.

sources.name: Bevan, Edwyn — A History of Egypt under the Ptolemaic Dynasty (Methuen, 1927)
Hölbl, Günther — A History of the Ptolemaic Empire (Routledge, 2001)

events.title: Rosetta Stone Decree Issued
events.detail: Ptolemy V issues a priestly decree at Memphis inscribed in three scripts — hieroglyphic, Demotic, and Greek — to assert his divinity and mandate priestly privileges. Rediscovered by Napoleon's troops in 1799 and decoded by Champollion in 1822, the Rosetta Stone becomes the key to understanding ancient Egyptian writing after 1,400 years of illiteracy.

sources.name: British Museum — The Rosetta Stone (EA24)
Parkinson, Richard — Cracking Codes: The Rosetta Stone and Decipherment (British Museum, 1999)

events.title: Cleopatra VII Allies with Julius Caesar
events.detail: Cleopatra VII Philopator — last active pharaoh of Egypt and the first Ptolemaic ruler to learn Egyptian — meets Julius Caesar during the Alexandrine War and forges a political alliance. She has a son by Caesar (Caesarion) and later allies with Mark Antony, gaining vast eastern territories. Their defeat at Actium in 31 BCE ends the Ptolemaic dynasty.

sources.name: Plutarch — Life of Caesar; Life of Antony (c. 100 CE)
Schiff, Stacy — Cleopatra: A Life (Little, Brown, 2010)

events.title: Roman Conquest — Cleopatra's Death Ends Pharaonic Egypt
events.detail: After the Battle of Actium, Octavian (later Augustus) invades Egypt. Mark Antony and Cleopatra VII commit suicide rather than face Roman captivity. Egypt becomes a personal province of the Roman Emperor — the breadbasket of Rome. Over 650 years of Roman and later Byzantine rule follow. Cleopatra's son Caesarion is executed.

sources.name: Plutarch — Life of Antony (c. 100 CE)
Bowman, Alan K. — Egypt after the Pharaohs (British Museum, 1986)

events.title: Arab Muslim Conquest — General Amr ibn al-As Takes Egypt
events.detail: Arab general Amr ibn al-As, commanding forces of Caliph Umar ibn al-Khattab, defeats the Byzantine garrison and captures the Fortress of Babylon (Old Cairo). Alexandria surrenders in 642 CE. Egypt rapidly converts over subsequent centuries; Arabic replaces Greek and Coptic in administration. Fustat (near modern Cairo) is founded as the new Muslim capital.

sources.name: Butler, Alfred J. — The Arab Conquest of Egypt (Clarendon Press, 1902)
Kennedy, Hugh — The Prophet and the Age of the Caliphates (Longman, 1986)

events.title: Fatimid General Jawhar Founds Cairo (al-Qahira)
events.detail: Fatimid general Jawhar al-Siqilli conquers Egypt and founds a new royal city named al-Qahira ('The Victorious') north of Fustat, destined to become one of the Islamic world's greatest capitals. The al-Azhar mosque and university — founded shortly after — becomes the world's oldest continuously operating university and the foremost center of Sunni Islamic learning.

sources.name: Sanders, Paula — Ritual, Politics, and the City in Fatimid Cairo (SUNY Press, 1994)
al-Maqrizi — Khitat (c. 1420 CE)

events.title: Saladin Establishes Ayyubid Sultanate
events.detail: Salah al-Din Yusuf ibn Ayyub (Saladin), initially sent as a Zengid commander, abolishes the Fatimid Caliphate, restores Sunni Shafi'i doctrine, and founds the Ayyubid Sultanate. In 1187, he defeats the Crusaders at the Battle of Hattin and recaptures Jerusalem — uniting Egypt, Syria, and parts of Arabia. He builds the Cairo Citadel.

sources.name: Lyons, Malcolm & Jackson, D.E.P. — Saladin: The Politics of the Holy War (Cambridge UP, 1982)
Ibn Shaddad — al-Nawadir al-Sultaniyya (c. 1215 CE)

events.title: Mamluk Sultans Overthrow Ayyubids
events.detail: Mamluk soldiers — enslaved Turkish and Circassian warriors trained as elite cavalry — overthrow the Ayyubid dynasty and establish the Mamluk Sultanate (1250–1517). In 1260, Mamluk Sultan Baybars defeats the Mongols at the Battle of Ain Jalut — the first major Mongol defeat — saving Egypt and the Islamic world from subjugation.

sources.name: Irwin, Robert — The Middle East in the Middle Ages (Croom Helm, 1986)
Amitai-Preiss, Reuven — Mongols and Mamluks (Cambridge UP, 1995)

events.title: Ottoman Sultan Selim I Conquers Egypt
events.detail: Ottoman Sultan Selim I defeats the Mamluk Sultanate at the Battle of Marj Dabiq (1516) and Battle of Ridanieh (1517), incorporating Egypt into the Ottoman Empire. Egypt becomes a province (eyalet) administered by a governor (pasha). The last Mamluk sultan, Tuman Bay II, is hanged at the Bab Zuwayla gate in Cairo. Mamluk beys continue as local administrators.

sources.name: Winter, Michael — Egyptian Society under Ottoman Rule (Routledge, 1992)
Holt, P.M. — Egypt and the Fertile Crescent 1516–1922 (Longman, 1966)

events.title: Napoleon's French Expedition to Egypt
events.detail: General Napoleon Bonaparte lands 38,000 troops at Aboukir Bay, defeats the Mamluks at the Battle of the Pyramids, and occupies Cairo. He brings 167 scholars (savants) who produce the Description de l'Égypte — 23 volumes documenting Egyptian antiquities and launching Egyptomania in Europe. British Admiral Nelson destroys the French fleet at Aboukir, trapping the expedition.

sources.name: Cole, Juan — Napoleon's Egypt: Invading the Middle East (Palgrave, 2007)
Description de l'Égypte (Commission des sciences et arts, 1809–1822)

events.title: Muhammad Ali Pasha Takes Power — Modernization Begins
events.detail: Albanian-born Ottoman officer Muhammad Ali Pasha outmaneuvers rivals and Ottomans to become ruler of Egypt. He massacres the Mamluk leadership at the Citadel in 1811, monopolizes trade, modernizes the army with French advisors, sends students to Europe, establishes factories, and builds a canal network. His dynasty rules Egypt until 1952.

sources.name: Fahmy, Khaled — All the Pasha's Men: Mehmed Ali, His Army and the Making of Modern Egypt (AUC Press, 1997)
Marsot, Afaf Lutfi al-Sayyid — Egypt in the Reign of Muhammad Ali (Cambridge UP, 1984)

events.title: Suez Canal Opens Under Khedive Ismail
events.detail: The Suez Canal — connecting the Mediterranean and Red Sea across 193 km of Egyptian desert — opens on 17 November 1869 after 10 years of construction. Designed by Ferdinand de Lesseps, it halves sailing distances between Europe and Asia. The lavish opening ceremony hosts Empress Eugénie of France, Emperor Franz Josef, and hundreds of dignitaries. Construction used forced corvée labor.

sources.name: Karabell, Zachary — Parting the Desert: The Creation of the Suez Canal (Knopf, 2003)
Suez Canal Authority Historical Records

events.title: British Occupation — Battle of Tel el-Kebir
events.detail: Following the Urabi nationalist revolt against Khedival misrule and Dual Control, Britain invades Egypt. The Battle of Tel el-Kebir (13 September 1882) defeats Colonel Ahmed Urabi's forces in 30 minutes. Britain occupies Egypt as a 'temporary' measure that lasts 72 years. Egypt remains nominally Ottoman until 1914, then becomes a British protectorate until 1922.

sources.name: Owen, Roger — Lord Cromer: Victorian Imperialist, Edwardian Proconsul (OUP, 2004)
Cole, Juan — Colonialism and Revolution in the Middle East (Princeton UP, 1993)

events.title: Egyptian Revolution of 1919 — Wafd Nationalist Uprising
events.detail: The arrest and deportation of nationalist leader Saad Zaghloul — who demanded independence at the Paris Peace Conference — triggers the 1919 Revolution: mass strikes, riots, and demonstrations across Egypt involving all social classes, including women. Britain grants Egypt nominal independence in 1922 and declares it a constitutional monarchy under Sultan Fuad I, renamed King.

sources.name: Beinin, Joel & Lockman, Zachary — Workers on the Nile (Princeton UP, 1987)
Saad Zaghloul — Diaries (Egyptian National Archive, 1919)

events.title: Free Officers Coup — King Farouk Ousted
events.detail: On 23 July 1952, a group of nationalist military officers led by Mohammed Naguib and Gamal Abdel Nasser overthrows King Farouk I, ending 150 years of the Muhammad Ali dynasty. Egypt is declared a republic in 1953. Nasser consolidates power by 1954, ousting Naguib, and embarks on land reform, industrialization, and pan-Arab nationalism.

sources.name: Nasser, Gamal Abdel — Egypt's Liberation: The Philosophy of the Revolution (1955)
Lacouture, Jean — Nasser (Knopf, 1973)

events.title: Suez Crisis — Nasser Nationalizes the Canal
events.detail: On 26 July 1956, Nasser announces the nationalization of the Suez Canal Company in retaliation for US and UK withdrawal of Aswan High Dam funding. Britain, France, and Israel invade in October (Operation Musketeer). US and Soviet pressure forces their withdrawal — Nasser emerges as a pan-Arab hero, the canal remains Egyptian, and British imperial prestige collapses.

sources.name: Kyle, Keith — Suez: Britain's End of Empire in the Middle East (I.B. Tauris, 2003)
al-Ahram — Nasser's Nationalization Speech transcript, July 27, 1956

events.title: Aswan High Dam Inaugurated
events.detail: The Aswan High Dam — built with Soviet assistance after US/UK withdrawal — is completed in 1970 after 11 years of construction. It controls Nile flooding, generates electricity for Egypt's industrialization, and enables year-round irrigation. But it also submerges Nubian villages and ancient sites (UNESCO rescued Abu Simbel temples by relocating them). Nasser dies days after the inauguration.

sources.name: Waterbury, John — The Egypt of Nasser and Sadat (Princeton UP, 1983)
UNESCO — Nubia Campaign Reports (1960–1980)

events.title: Six-Day War — Israel Seizes Sinai
events.detail: On 5 June 1967, Israel launches preemptive air strikes destroying the Egyptian Air Force on the ground. Israeli ground forces cross the Suez Canal and occupy the entire Sinai Peninsula within days. Egypt loses 10,000+ soldiers and massive military equipment. The defeat — the 'Naksa' (setback) — shatters pan-Arabism and leads Nasser to temporarily offer his resignation.

sources.name: Oren, Michael — Six Days of War (OUP, 2002)
El-Gamasy, General Mohamed — The October War (AUC Press, 1993)

events.title: October War (Yom Kippur) — Egypt Crosses the Canal
events.detail: On 6 October 1973 (Yom Kippur / Ramadan 10), Egyptian forces cross the Suez Canal in a surprise assault, breaching the Bar-Lev Line and advancing into Sinai in the first hours of war. Initial Egyptian success restores national honor lost in 1967. Israeli counterattacks encircle the Third Army; a ceasefire is brokered. Sadat leverages partial victory into peace negotiations.

sources.name: El-Gamasy, General Mohamed — The October War (AUC Press, 1993)
Shazly, General Saad — The Crossing of the Suez (American Mideast Research, 1980)
Oren, Michael & Arad, Yitzhak — The Yom Kippur War (Doubleday, 2004)

events.title: Camp David Accords — Egypt–Israel Peace Framework
events.detail: US President Carter brokers secret negotiations between President Sadat and Israeli PM Begin at Camp David, resulting in two frameworks: a peace treaty roadmap and principles for Palestinian autonomy. Egypt and Israel sign a formal peace treaty in March 1979 — Israel returns the Sinai, Egypt becomes the first Arab state to recognize Israel, and Sadat and Begin share the Nobel Peace Prize.

sources.name: Camp David Accords (US Government, September 17, 1978)
Quandt, William — Camp David: Peacemaking and Politics (Brookings, 1986)

events.title: Sadat Assassinated — Mubarak Assumes Power
events.detail: President Anwar Sadat is assassinated on 6 October 1981 at a military parade in Cairo by Islamist army officers led by Khaled Islambouli, members of Egyptian Islamic Jihad who condemned his peace with Israel and crackdown on opposition. Vice President Hosni Mubarak assumes the presidency and imposes emergency law that remains in force for 30 years.

sources.name: Sullivan, Denis — Private Voluntary Organizations in Egypt (University Press of Florida, 1994)
al-Ahram — October 7, 1981 front-page coverage

events.title: Mubarak's 30-Year Authoritarian Rule
events.detail: Hosni Mubarak rules Egypt from 1981 to 2011 under perpetual Emergency Law. His regime maintains the peace with Israel, receives billions in US military aid annually, and oversees economic liberalization that enriches a crony elite while leaving millions in poverty. Torture, political imprisonment, and rigged elections define the era. His son Gamal is groomed for succession.

sources.name: Osman, Tarek — Egypt on the Brink (Yale UP, 2010)
Human Rights Watch — Egypt annual reports 1990–2011

events.title: Tahrir Square Revolution — Mubarak Falls
events.detail: Inspired by Tunisia's Jasmine Revolution, Egyptians flood Tahrir Square on 25 January 2011 ('Police Day') calling for an end to Mubarak's rule. Over 18 days, millions protest despite security crackdowns that kill 840 people. On 11 February, the Supreme Council of the Armed Forces (SCAF) announces Mubarak has resigned. The world watches live on Al Jazeera.

sources.name: Khalil, Ashraf — Liberation Square: Inside the Egyptian Revolution (St. Martin's, 2011)
Al Jazeera — 18 Days of Tahrir live coverage (January–February 2011)
Human Rights Watch — Egypt: Bloody Crackdown on Protests (2011)

events.title: Mohammed Morsi Elected — Muslim Brotherhood Reaches Power
events.detail: Muslim Brotherhood candidate Mohammed Morsi wins Egypt's first free presidential election with 51.7% of votes, defeating Mubarak-era PM Ahmed Shafik. Morsi issues a controversial constitutional declaration placing himself above judicial review in November 2012. Mass opposition protests resume at Tahrir Square. He pushes through an Islamist-drafted constitution in a rushed referendum.

sources.name: Egyptian Presidential Election Commission — Official Results 2012
BBC — Egypt election: Morsi wins presidential vote (24 June 2012)

events.title: Sisi Military Coup — Morsi Deposed; Raba'a Massacre
events.detail: On 3 July 2013, Defense Minister General Abdel Fattah el-Sisi announces Morsi's removal after millions take to the streets in the June 30 protests. On 14 August, security forces clear pro-Morsi sit-ins at Raba'a al-Adawiya and al-Nahda squares — Human Rights Watch documents at least 817 killed in what it calls one of the world's largest mass killings of demonstrators.

sources.name: Human Rights Watch — All According to Plan: The Raba'a Massacre (2014)
Egyptian Interior Ministry — official statement on Raba'a dispersal (August 2013)
Amnesty International — Egypt: Deadly crackdown on protest (2013)

events.confidenceReason: Death toll disputed — government says ~200–250, HRW documents 817+, some estimates exceed 1,000

events.title: Sisi Elected President with 96% Vote
events.detail: Abdel Fattah el-Sisi wins the 2014 presidential election with 96.9% of votes in a contest marred by low turnout (47.5%), mass Brotherhood boycott, and absence of meaningful opposition. He is re-elected in 2018 (97%) and 2023 (89.6%) in elections described by international monitors as non-competitive. A 2019 constitutional amendment allows Sisi to remain president until 2030.

sources.name: Egyptian High Presidential Election Commission 2014 results
Carter Center — Egypt Presidential Election Assessment (2014)

events.title: New Administrative Capital Announced
events.detail: Egypt announces the construction of a new administrative capital city 45 km east of Cairo, designed to house 6.5 million people and relocate government ministries, parliament, and the presidential palace. The $58 billion mega-project — partly funded by UAE investment and built by Egyptian Army enterprises — is seen as a prestige project; critics question its cost during austerity.

sources.name: Egypt's New Administrative Capital Company official announcements
Reuters — Egypt's new capital: a Pharaonic project for Sisi's legacy (2020)

events.title: IMF $12 Billion Loan — EGP Float and Austerity
events.detail: Egypt secures a $12 billion three-year IMF Extended Fund Facility in November 2016. As a condition, it floats the Egyptian pound (losing half its value overnight), cuts energy subsidies, and raises taxes. The EGP depreciates from 8.8 to 18 per USD. Inflation spikes above 30%. The program is presented as essential structural reform; critics note its heavy burden on the poor.

sources.name: IMF Press Release No. 16/501 — Egypt Extended Arrangement (November 11, 2016)
World Bank — Egypt Economic Monitor (2017)

events.title: Egypt Joins BRICS+ — Geopolitical Diversification
events.detail: Egypt formally joins the BRICS+ grouping on 1 January 2024 alongside Saudi Arabia, UAE, Iran, and Ethiopia, following an invitation at the Johannesburg BRICS Summit in August 2023. Egypt views membership as a hedge against Western conditionality on aid and loans, and a signal of strategic diversification. Egypt simultaneously maintains its US security partnership and IMF program.

sources.name: BRICS Johannesburg II Declaration, August 23, 2023
Reuters — Egypt, Saudi Arabia, UAE, Iran join BRICS (January 1, 2024)

events.title: IMF Expands Egypt Loan to $8 Billion; Second EGP Devaluation
events.detail: In March 2024, the IMF expands Egypt's Extended Fund Facility to $8 billion (from $3 billion) as Egypt implements a flexible exchange rate — the EGP plunges from 31 to 50 per USD, another ~40% devaluation. Separately, Abu Dhabi's sovereign wealth fund ADQ announces a $35 billion investment in Egypt's Ras El-Hekma coastal development zone, providing critical forex relief.

sources.name: IMF Press Release — Egypt: Extended Arrangement March 2024
Reuters — Egypt pound falls 40% after IMF deal (March 6, 2024)
